steps.title: Identify what “generalization about the kind of study” means
steps.content:
The task is not to report a specific finding about “In My Life” or the 18.9% probability.

The word “generalization” signals that the student must step back and describe the type or method of research itself—what approach Glickman, Brown, and Song used and what kinds of questions it can answer.

The notes reveal they applied statistical methods to study authorship questions, and this pattern (the method applied to the field) is what needs to be generalized.

steps.title: Match the generalization to the choices
steps.content:
Choice D states: “Researchers have used statistical methods to address questions of authorship within the field of music.” This generalizes the methodology (statistical methods) and the research focus (authorship questions) without getting trapped in case-specific details.

Choices A, B, and C all describe specific details—the probability for McCartney, the conclusion about “In My Life,” or the likelihood that Lennon wrote the verse—rather than stepping back to characterize the kind of study.

They report findings, not the research approach itself. That matches choice D — D is correct.
